metadata.description: Published power analysis examples from verified online calculators, software documentation, university resources, and peer-reviewed papers
sources: G*Power software documentation and PMC paper (PMC8441096)
UCLA OARC G*Power data analysis examples (stats.oarc.ucla.edu)
Minitab power and sample size documentation (support.minitab.com)
PMC papers on sample size and effect size (PMC7745163, PMC6955025)

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial (J Educ Eval Health Prof, 2021)
examples.scenario: Comparing analgesic efficacy of Drug A (traditional) vs Drug B (newly developed) using Visual Analog Scale pain scores at 6 hours post-operation
examples.notes: Cohen's medium effect size. Most commonly cited G*Power example.

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Pilot study with 10 patients per group comparing Drug A vs Drug B pain relief. Drug A: mean=7, SD=3; Drug B: mean=5, SD=2
examples.notes: Effect size computed from pilot study means and SDs

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Post-hoc power analysis: Completed trial with 30 patients per group comparing analgesics. Drug A: mean=7, SD=3; Drug B: mean=5, SD=2

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Weight loss study: determining sample size to detect a small effect in weight before vs after a diet program
examples.notes: Cohen's small effect size for paired design

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Pilot study examining diet program effectiveness on weight loss. Pre-diet: mean=66kg, SD=12kg; Post-diet: mean=62kg, SD=11kg; correlation=0.7

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Post-hoc power: Completed weight loss trial with 100 participants. Pre-diet: mean=66kg, SD=12kg; Post-diet: mean=62kg, SD=11kg; correlation=0.7

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Comparing analgesic efficacy across four drugs (A, B, C, D) measuring VAS pain scores at 6 hours post-operation
examples.notes: Cohen's medium effect size for ANOVA (f=0.25). 45 per group.

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Pilot study (n=5/group) comparing four analgesics. Drug A: mean=2, SD=2; Drug B: mean=4, SD=1; Drug C: mean=5, SD=1; Drug D: mean=5, SD=2. Pooled SD=1.58
examples.notes: Large effect size from pilot data. 6 per group.

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Post-hoc: Completed four-drug comparison trial with 20 patients per group. Drug A: mean=2, SD=2; Drug B: mean=4, SD=1; Drug C: mean=5, SD=1; Drug D: mean=5, SD=2

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Investigating correlation between height and weight in pediatric patients

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Post-hoc: Completed study of height-weight correlation in 50 pediatric patients with r=0.5

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Comparing effectiveness of Treatment A (traditional, 30% neuralgia) vs Treatment B (new, 10% neuralgia) for preventing post-herpetic neuralgia

examples.source: PMC8441096 - G*Power software tutorial
examples.scenario: Post-hoc: Completed trial comparing post-herpetic neuralgia. Treatment A: 31/101 (30.7%), Treatment B: 9/98 (9.2%)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Julious (2004) Example 2.1.1
examples.scenario: Superiority trial for continuous outcome, validated against Julious (2004)
examples.notes: Validated against Julious SA (2004) Sample Sizes for Clinical Trials

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Pocock (1983)
examples.scenario: Superiority trial for continuous outcome, validated against Pocock (1983)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Julious (2004) Example 3.4.3
examples.scenario: Equivalence trial for continuous outcome, validated against Julious (2004)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Julious (2004) Example 4.1.1
examples.scenario: Non-inferiority trial for continuous outcome, validated against Julious (2004)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Pocock (1983)
examples.scenario: Superiority trial for binary outcome (90% vs 95%), validated against Pocock (1983)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Julious (2009) Example 12.1
examples.scenario: Equivalence trial for binary outcome (p=70%, margin=10%), validated against Julious (2009)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Julious (2009) Example 12.1
examples.scenario: Equivalence trial for binary outcome (p=70%, margin=20%), validated against Julious (2009)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Julious (2009) Example 12.1
examples.scenario: Equivalence trial for binary outcome (p=90%, margin=5%), validated against Julious (2009)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Donner (1984) Example 3
examples.scenario: Non-inferiority trial, binary outcome (p=80%, margin=10%), validated against Donner (1984)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Blackwelder (1982) Table 3
examples.scenario: Non-inferiority trial, binary outcome (p=90%, margin=20%), validated against Blackwelder (1982)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Blackwelder (1982) Table 3
examples.scenario: Non-inferiority trial, binary (p_standard=60%, p_experimental=50%, margin=20%), Blackwelder (1982)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Pocock (1983)
examples.scenario: Non-inferiority trial, binary outcome (p=70%, margin=10%), validated against Pocock (1983)

examples.source: Sealed Envelope validation - Pocock (2003)
examples.scenario: Non-inferiority trial, binary outcome (p=85%, margin=15%), validated against Pocock (2003)

examples.source: UCLA OARC - One-way ANOVA Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Comparing teaching methods for 4 statistics classes. Group means: 550, 598, 598, 646; common SD=80; large effect size

examples.source: UCLA OARC - One-way ANOVA Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Comparing teaching methods, medium effect size. Group means: 550, 580, 580, 610; common SD=80
examples.notes: Note: UCLA labels this 'medium' but f=0.75 is actually large by Cohen's convention (medium=0.25)

examples.source: UCLA OARC - Two-group independent sample t-test power analysis
examples.scenario: Dietician comparing two diets for diabetic patients, testing blood glucose after 6 weeks. Diet A: SD=15; Diet B: SD=17; expected difference=10 mg/dl

examples.source: UCLA OARC - Two-group independent sample t-test power analysis
examples.scenario: Post-hoc power analysis for diet study with 30 per group, difference=10, SDs=15 and 17

examples.source: UCLA OARC - Two-group independent sample t-test power analysis
examples.scenario: Post-hoc: Diet study with unequal groups (40 vs 20), difference=10, SDs=15 and 17

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Two Independent Proportions Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Testing proportion difference of 0.15 (p1=0.30 vs p2=0.15) using Pearson chi-square, two-sided

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Two Independent Proportions Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Same scenario (p1=0.30 vs p2=0.15) using Fisher's exact test, two-sided

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Two Independent Proportions Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Proportion comparison (p1=0.30 vs p2=0.15) using Fisher's exact, one-sided

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Two Independent Proportions Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Unequal groups (1:2 ratio), proportion comparison (p1=0.30 vs p2=0.15), one-sided

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Power Analysis for Paired Sample t-test
examples.scenario: Weight loss program: mean difference=5 lbs, SD=5, correlation=0.5

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Power Analysis for Paired Sample t-test
examples.scenario: Weight loss program: mean difference=5 lbs, SD=5, correlation=0.5, high power

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Power Analysis for Paired Sample t-test
examples.scenario: Manual dexterity study: mean difference=5 sec, SD=10, correlation=0.5, n=35

examples.source: UCLA OARC SAS - Power Analysis for Paired Sample t-test
examples.scenario: Paired test with unequal SDs: diff=5, SD1=7, SD2=12, correlation=0.5

examples.source: UCLA OARC - G*Power Correlation Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Comparing two soil test correlations (old test r=0.89, new test r=0.76) with criterion

examples.source: UCLA OARC - G*Power Correlation Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Same soil test comparison, higher power target

examples.source: UCLA OARC - Multiple Regression Power Analysis
examples.scenario: School district: effect of mother's education on English proficiency, controlling for 4 other variables. Full model R²=0.48, incremental R²=0.03

examples.source: UCLA OARC - Multiple Regression Power Analysis
examples.scenario: Same study: testing language spoken at home (2 df categorical variable), incremental R²=0.025
examples.notes: Final recommended sample for the whole study to cover both hypotheses

examples.source: Minitab - Power and Sample Size for 2-Sample t
examples.scenario: Healthcare consultant comparing patient satisfaction ratings between two hospitals

examples.source: Minitab - Power and Sample Size for 1-Sample t
examples.scenario: Economist testing whether monthly family energy costs changed from previous mean of $200. Difference to detect: $100, SD: $150

examples.source: Minitab - Power and Sample Size for 2 Proportions
examples.scenario: Testing detectability of difference in summer job rates between male and female students, baseline proportion 60%
examples.notes: Can detect approximately 7% difference in either direction with n=1000 per group

examples.source: Minitab - Power and Sample Size for Paired t
examples.scenario: Weight-loss program manager: detect difference of 3 lbs with SD of paired differences = 5 lbs

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23 (bookdown.org/pdr_higgins)
examples.scenario: New drug to reduce hemoglobin A1c in type 2 diabetes over 1 year. Placebo mean=9.0, Treatment mean=7.0, SD=3.0

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23
examples.scenario: Same A1c study with budget constraint of n=25 per arm

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23
examples.scenario: Unblinded pilot of enema treatment for transplant-waiting patients to reduce serum creatinine. Expected reduction=1.0 g/dL, SD=2.0

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23
examples.scenario: Bilateral skin treatment application, large effect (d=0.8), two-sample design

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23
examples.scenario: Same bilateral skin treatment as paired design (d=0.8)
examples.notes: Paired design much more efficient than two-sample (15 vs 52)

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23
examples.scenario: Blood pressure reduction study: expected 10mmHg reduction, SD=20mmHg, control group fixed at n=40
examples.notes: Approximately 4:1 ratio needed when control group is constrained

examples.source: Reproducible Medical Research with R - Ch 23
examples.scenario: Post-hoc: Blood pressure study with n1=40, n2=160, d=0.5

examples.source: Select Statistical Consultants - Two Proportions Calculator
examples.scenario: Supermarket testing new marketing promotion: current purchase rate 15%, desired rate 25%

examples.source: PMC7745163 - Sample size, power, effect size revisited (Biochem Med, 2020)
examples.scenario: Comparing total cholesterol: Group 1 mean=6.5 mmol/L (SD=0.5), Group 2 mean=5.2 mmol/L (SD=0.8), n=30 each

examples.source: PMC7745163 - Sample size, power, effect size revisited
examples.scenario: SNP case-control genetic association study: minor allele frequency 15% in cases vs 7% in controls

examples.source: PMC7745163 - Sample size, power, effect size revisited
examples.scenario: Population survey with population size 10,000, 95% CI and 5% margin of error

examples.source: PMC7745163 - Sample size, power, effect size revisited
examples.scenario: Diagnostic accuracy study: desired sensitivity=0.85, specificity=0.90, 95% CI with max error=0.05

examples.source: PMC7745163 - Sample size, power, effect size revisited
examples.scenario: Relationship between effect size and required sample size at power=0.80 (small effect)

examples.source: PMC7745163 - Sample size, power, effect size revisited
examples.scenario: Relationship between effect size and required sample size at power=0.80 (large effect)

examples.source: Penn State STAT 509 - Sample Size for Equivalence (continuous)
examples.scenario: Asthma equivalence trial measuring FEV1: equivalence zone=+-0.1L, assumed true difference=0.05L, SD=0.75L
examples.notes: Equivalence trials require much larger samples than superiority trials

examples.source: Penn State STAT 509 / PMC6955025 - Non-inferiority trial (binary)
examples.scenario: Treatment success non-inferiority: control=70%, experimental=65%, margin=5%

examples.source: PMC6955025 - Non-inferiority trial (binary, rheumatoid arthritis)
examples.scenario: Disease activity-guided dose reduction vs usual care for rheumatoid arthritis: DR=17% flare, UC=14% flare, margin=20%

examples.source: Penn State STAT 509 - Non-inferiority trial (time-to-event)
examples.scenario: Time to infection: control failure rate=20%, non-inferiority threshold=25%, 1 year follow-up

examples.source: nQuery / SAS Paper 4675-2020
examples.scenario: Comparing two drugs for pain score changes. Drug A mean change=5, Drug B mean change=4.5, SD=1.195

examples.source: NCSS PASS documentation - Chow, Shao, Wang, Lokhnygina (2018)
examples.scenario: Two-sided two-sample t-test with delta=0.05, sigma=0.1
examples.notes: From Chow, Shao, Wang, and Lokhnygina (2018) textbook example

examples.source: NCSS PASS documentation - Machin et al. (1997) p.35
examples.scenario: Two-sided test: mean difference=5, common SD=10, 90% power
examples.notes: From Machin, Campbell, Fayers, Pinol (1997) textbook, same as Minitab-001

examples.source: NCSS PASS documentation - Two proportions
examples.scenario: Two-sided test comparing P1=0.7 vs P2=0.6 at alpha=0.01, 95% power

examples.source: NCSS PASS documentation - Effect size approach
examples.scenario: Small effect size d=0.2, detecting with 80% power
examples.notes: Cohen's small effect size requires very large sample

examples.source: UVA Library - Power and Sample Size for Logistic Regression
examples.scenario: Binary predictor logistic regression with interaction. P(y=1): x=1,z=0 -> 0.85; x=1,z=1 -> 0.50; x=0,z=0 -> 0.40; x=0,z=1 -> 0.30

examples.source: UVA Library - Power and Sample Size for Logistic Regression
examples.scenario: Same logistic model: finding n for 90% power at alpha=0.05

examples.source: PowerAndSampleSize.com - Two-sample equivalence for means
examples.scenario: Means equivalence: mu_A=5, mu_B=4, equivalence margin=5, SD=10

examples.source: PowerAndSampleSize.com - Two-sample equivalence for proportions
examples.scenario: Proportion equivalence: p_A=0.65, p_B=0.85, margin=0.05

examples.source: ScaleStatistics.com - Chi-square sample size
examples.scenario: Marketing agency testing two packaging versions (A vs B). Effect size w=0.3, power=0.8, df=1
